cataloging_subject.label: The Life of St. Norbert
cataloging_subject.scope_note: John Capgrave's 4,109-line Middle English rhyme-royal life of Saint Norbert, completed in Assumption week 1440, including its dedicatory prologue and closing envoy. The cataloging subject is not Smetana's 1977 scholarly book, the 1983 OTA text, the 2023 U-M encoding, the repository XML, physical manuscript HM 55, or the unrelated Latin breviary fragment preserved as HM 55's lifted former pastedown.

catalog_summary.title: The Life of St. Norbert
catalog_summary.author: John Capgrave
catalog_summary.region: England; the surviving witness is linguistically localized to Norfolk, with possible production at Lynn and dedication to the abbot of West Dereham; Huntington describes the presentation manuscript as evidently autograph

notes: The preferred modern English title follows Smetana and DIMEV; manuscript f. 1r is identified by its opening rather than treated as evidence for this exact editorial title form.
This is the heading in the 2023 U-M encoding, not necessarily a manuscript-supplied title.

titles.value: The life of St. John Norbert
notes: This is the current Oxford repository title for OTA 0162. Its St. John Norbert wording is retained as a legacy access title, not adopted as the preferred identification.
A cataloging uniform title carried in the U-M header; it does not identify the exact Latin recension or physical exemplar translated by Capgrave.

titles.value: Ioye, grace & pees, loue, feith & charite
Aftyr þis lyf, I pray God send us heuene!
notes: The following Latin Feliciter is modeled separately as a terminal formula rather than counted as a 4,110th verse line.

agents.name: John Capgrave
notes: The poem names Ion Capgraue as the friar who translated the story and completed it in 1440. Huntington describes the presentation copy made by Capgrave as evidently autograph and identifies interlinear corrections as his; DIMEV independently identifies Capgrave as author and HM 55 as the witness.

agents.name: Cyril Lawrence Smetana
notes: Editor of the 1977 PIMS volume used by the OTA/U-M source chain; not the medieval author.
The source XML's top-level AUTHOR elements describe the modern edition and manuscript repository; they must not displace Capgrave's directly attested medieval authorship.

scope.description: Middle English verse rendering of a Latin Vita Sancti Norberti, with Capgrave's prologue, envoy, additions, and omissions

notes: Capgrave says that he will translate the story and identifies himself as the friar who translated it. The exact Latin recension and exemplar remain unresolved.

scope.description: Smetana's 1977 source edition of the medieval work, not authorship of the work itself

assertion.notes: Lines 4104–4108 explicitly name Capgrave, Assumption week, and the year 1440.

notes: The secure completion date does not resolve the earlier-phase proposal summarized by Gribbin: Peter J. Lucas argued that Capgrave may have begun the work before 1422 and completed and dedicated it in 1440.

date_statements.display: 1440 presentation manuscript, described by Huntington as evidently autograph
notes: HM 55 is the 1440 presentation copy that Huntington describes as evidently autograph; DIMEV records it as the sole known witness. Composition and first attestation therefore coincide chronologically but remain distinct descriptive claims.

places.label: Norfolk, England
notes: DIMEV reports the LALME/eLALME localization for HM 55; this is a linguistic claim about the surviving witness, not an automatic composition-place claim.

places.label: Lynn, Norfolk
notes: Huntington proposes possible manuscript production at the Augustinian house at Lynn.

scope.description: Language of the sole known witness HM 55, which Huntington describes as evidently autograph

assertion.notes: The statement is witness-specific despite the whole-work discovery scope.

notes: Do not convert the witness's Norfolk dialect localization into a secure place of composition.

scope.description: Possible production context of the presentation manuscript that Huntington describes as evidently autograph

assertion.notes: The repository says possibly the Augustinian house at Lynn.

notes: This is not cataloged as a confirmed place of composition.

scope.description: Institutional destination and patronage context named in the dedication and envoy

notes: Capgrave sends the book to John Wygenhale, abbot of Derham near Stoke, identified by scholarship and the repository as West Dereham. This patronage location is not proof that the poem was composed there.

form_statements.description: A 4,109-line poem in 587 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with a 70-line prologue, 37 numbered chapters, and a 14-line envoy. The final Latin Feliciter is outside the verse count.

genre_statements.label: Religious translation
notes: The generic Latin Vita source is secure, while its exact recension and exemplar remain unresolved.

subject_statements.label: Norbert of Xanten, Saint, Archbishop of Magdeburg (d. 1134)
Religious orders and the Rule of Saint Augustine
notes: The prologue explicitly links Capgrave's Augustinian order and Norbert's followers under Augustine's rule.

summaries.value: John Capgrave's Middle English verse life narrates Norbert's conversion, religious vocation, foundation and governance of the Premonstratensian order, ecclesiastical career, miracles, and sanctity. Capgrave closely translates a Latin Vita while reshaping it selectively for a 4,109-line rhyme-royal poem addressed to John Wygenhale, abbot of West Dereham. A dedicatory prologue connects Capgrave's Augustinian community with Norbert's order, and a two-stanza envoy names the recipient, translator, and completion in Assumption week 1440.

DIMEV records Huntington HM 55 as the poem's sole known witness at ff. 1r–59v; Huntington describes it as a presentation copy made by Capgrave and evidently autograph. The codex also preserves a separately lifted former pastedown with a Latin breviary fragment; that fragment is physical context, not a content part or language facet of The Life of St. Norbert. The five public Huntington images are selected surfaces rather than a complete codex surrogate.

CME90162 represents only the medieval poem and a short project argument, not Smetana's full 1977 volume. U-M revised an incomplete OCR-like OTA capture against the edition, restoring about 200 omitted lines and adding chapter, line, page, and folio markup. The edition, encoding, and manuscript remain separate descriptive layers.

extent.description: The represented item contains one modern prose editorial frame outside the intrinsic work and 39 analytical verse sections: the prologue, 37 chapters, and envoy. The intrinsic verse consists of 4,109 consecutively numbered lines: a 70-line prologue, 4,025 narrative lines, and a 14-line envoy embedded at the end of chapter 37. At seven lines per rhyme-royal stanza, this is 587 stanzas. The XML has 4,110 L elements because it also assigns N=4110 to the non-verse Latin terminal formula Feliciter; it encodes no LG stanza elements. It has 38 DIV2 containers, since the editorial frame is not a DIV2 and the envoy remains inside chapter 37 rather than receiving its own division.

content_parts.label: Modern U-M editorial frame
content_parts.summary: Modern English project framing precedes the medieval poem: two headings identify the electronic edition and work, an opener supplies the project byline and February 2023 dateline, and a prose argument explains the OTA-derived text, U-M repairs, and structural markup.

locators.value: BODY/HEAD[1–2], OPENER/BYLINE/DATELINE, and ARGUMENT/P before DIV1

notes: This prose is modern representation-level paratext, not part of Capgrave's medieval work or one of its 39 intrinsic verse sections.

content_parts.label: Dedicatory prologue
content_parts.summary: Capgrave addresses his patron, defends his verse-making, announces his translation of Norbert's life, links his own Augustinian community with Norbert's order, and offers the book for reception into the patron's fraternity.
notes: The XML creates one DIV2 TYPE=prologue. item_count is zero because the work's 37 numbered items are its chapters; the prologue remains a separately counted verse section.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Norbert in 37 chapters
content_parts.summary: The numbered narrative follows Norbert from his worldly youth and sudden conversion through his religious foundations, preaching, church offices, conflicts, miracles, death, and posthumous signs. The analytical boundary ends immediately before the two-stanza envoy within chapter 37.

locators.value: L 71–4095; 37 DIV2 TYPE=chapter elements with N=1–21, xxii, 23–37

notes: The physical and printed end folio/page is shared with the envoy; the line boundary prevents the narrative part from claiming the whole terminal surface exclusively.

content_parts.label: Envoy to the book
content_parts.summary: Capgrave sends the little book to the abbot of West Dereham, names John Wygenhale, identifies himself as translator, and dates completion to Assumption week 1440 before the final prayer for heaven.
notes: DIMEV treats the envoy as part of record-2973 and cross-refers a separate legacy description to that record. The analytical part does not imply a distinct independently transmitted work.
The envoy is not a separate XML division; it occupies the final fourteen lines of chapter 37.

content_parts.label: Terminal formula Feliciter
content_parts.summary: The single Latin word Feliciter follows the poem and is associated with Capgrave's signature and trefoil on f. 59v.
notes: Although the XML numbers Feliciter as L 4110, scholarship reports 4,109 verse lines and the word is not treated as a verse line or stanza.

related_works.title: Vita Sancti Norberti
notes: The existence of a Latin Vita source is secure, but the exact recension, BHL number, and physical exemplar used by Capgrave have not been verified. BHL 6248–6250 should remain a field of candidates rather than being collapsed into one asserted source.

evidence.citation: Publication-bound University of Michigan CME90162 XML.
evidence.locator: HEADER; ARGUMENT; prologue L 1–70; chapters L 71–4095; envoy L 4096–4109; Feliciter L 4110
evidence.summary: Supports the preferred and uniform titles, Capgrave's author-translator responsibility, Middle English language, Norbert subject, 37-chapter structure, dedication, Assumption-week 1440 completion, printed-page and manuscript-folio locators, and the separate numbered terminal formula.
notes: The linked lineage evidence records the repository path and exact XML fixity.

evidence.citation: Deterministic structural inspection of publication-bound CME90162 XML.
evidence.locator: BODY descendants DIV2, L, PB, and MILESTONE
evidence.summary: Counts 37 chapter DIV2 elements, one prologue DIV2, 4,110 L elements numbered 1–4110, 135 PB elements, and 116 folio MILESTONE elements. Intrinsic verse is L 1–4109; L 4110 is the Latin terminal Feliciter. PB 31 repeats while 32 is absent; folio 25v repeats while 16r, 30v, and 35v are absent from the encoded milestone sequence. These are encoding metrics, not silent corrections to source pagination or foliation.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive repository record and exact plain-text bitstream for ota:0162.
evidence.summary: Supports the legacy OTA title, identifier family, poem-only representation, folio labels, and ending, while preserving the incomplete OCR-like predecessor separately from U-M's repaired encoding.

evidence.citation: National Library of Ireland catalog record for Capgrave's The Life of St. Norbert, ed. Cyril Lawrence Smetana (1977).
evidence.summary: Supports the title, Capgrave's authorship, Smetana's editorship, Middle English verse translation classification, 1977 edition identity, and HM 55 source witness.

evidence.citation: Google Books limited preview of Smetana's 1977 edition.
evidence.summary: Directly verifies Smetana's title-page role and the source volume's contents, including the edited Life beginning on p. 19 and the separate introduction, comments, glossary, bibliography, citation list, and index.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library's official description and selected images for mssHM 55.
evidence.summary: Supports the 1440 presentation manuscript, Huntington's description of it as evidently autograph, England and possible Lynn production context, John Wygenhale dedication, work locus ff. 1r–59v, Capgrave's hand and Feliciter signature, and exclusion of the separate Latin breviary fragment. It does not independently establish uniqueness.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record-2973 / IMEV and NIMEV 1805.
evidence.summary: Supports Capgrave's authorship, the incipit and final verse, saints' life and envoy classifications, seven-line ababbcc stanza form, unique HM 55 witness at ff. 1–59v, Smetana edition, and Norfolk linguistic localization.

evidence.citation: Sarah James, “Capgrave, Bokenham, and Late Medieval Saints' Lives,” in A Companion to Fifteenth-Century English Poetry, ed. Julia Boffey and A. S. G. Edwards (Cambridge: D. S. Brewer, 2013), 99–112, author accepted manuscript, ISBN 9781843843535.
evidence.summary: Supports the 4,109-line rhyme-royal form, 1440 completion, dedication to John Wygenhale of West Dereham, close translation of an early Latin Vita, Capgrave's selective additions and omissions, and the framing prologue and envoy.

evidence.citation: Joseph A. Gribbin, “The Date of John Capgrave's Life of St Norbert,” appendix in The Premonstratensian Order in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2000), 248–250.
evidence.locator: Publisher chapter summary and bibliographic metadata
evidence.summary: Supports reporting the pre-1422 composition theory as Peter J. Lucas's scholarly proposal, alongside the securely attested completion and dedication in 1440; it does not turn the proposed early phase into a confirmed date.

open_questions.question: Which exact Latin Vita Sancti Norberti recension and physical exemplar underlie Capgrave's translation?
next_steps: Consult Smetana's full introduction and later source studies for explicit recension and exemplar claims.
Collate Capgrave's chapter sequence, additions, and omissions against the BHL 6248–6250 Vita tradition before assigning a specific BHL identifier.
Preserve any abstract recension and any proposed physical exemplar as distinct source layers.

open_questions.question: Does the secure Assumption-week 1440 completion encompass the whole composition, or did Capgrave begin a version before 1422?
next_steps: Read Peter J. Lucas's full chronology argument and subsequent scholarship rather than relying on preview summaries.
Model any substantiated early phase as a separate date assertion without weakening the explicit 1440 completion.

open_questions.question: Can the work's place of composition be established more narrowly than England without conflating witness dialect, possible manuscript production at Lynn, and dedication to West Dereham?
next_steps: Review paleographical, dialectal, biographical, and institutional scholarship on Capgrave's location in 1440.
Keep Norfolk dialect localization, Lynn production proposal, and West Dereham patronage as separate assertions unless direct evidence links one to composition.

notes: The cataloging subject is the medieval intellectual work; Smetana's edition, OTA deposit, U-M revision, repository artifact, manuscript, and selected images remain separately bound lineage entities.

The XML's AUTHOR fields are bibliographic header fields for the modern edition and repository, not reliable work-level authorship fields. Capgrave's authorship and translation are established by the poem, manuscript catalog, DIMEV, and scholarship.
The extent distinguishes 4,109 intrinsic verse lines from 4,110 XML L elements. Feliciter is retained as a terminal formula rather than silently discarded or counted as verse.

The XML has a duplicate PB 31 and no PB 32, plus a duplicate folio milestone 25v and no milestones for 16r, 30v, or 35v. These anomalies are documented as representation facts and are not silently normalized into source pagination or foliation.
The language facet remains Middle English. The single Latin terminal word and the separate Latin breviary fragment do not make the cataloged work bilingual.

Commercial and redistribution decisions must use the linked lineage rights records; public-domain status of the medieval work does not erase rights or terms attached to modern editing, encoding, or photography.
